WTI — the insulated crude¶

Sample: 2017-11-21 → 2026-04-17 · 2 169 trading days · ICE WTI M1-M12 pre-rolled settlements (WBS 1!-ICE … WBS 12!-ICE).

What WTI is in the market: Cushing-delivered US crude, pipeline-connected, historically isolated from global flows. Since the 2015 US export-ban repeal it sits in the Atlantic basin via USGC loadings, but it still trades with more US-domestic DNA than Brent — refinery outages in PADD 3, pipeline capacity, SPR releases all move WTI before they move Brent. That makes WTI the natural benchmark for reading US-specific supply stress, and the short end of Brent-WTI spread (covered in Cross-Market) for reading basin-arb economics.

Sample-length caveat — up front, not in a footer. In-sample window ends 2024-12-31; OOS is ≈16 months (≈333 trading days). All regime-frequency claims below carry wide error bars at this sample length. Direction is unambiguous; level is descriptive of the current episode, not predictive of a distribution.

What this notebook argues:

  1. Backwardation default, same as LSGO and Brent. 68.2% of days print M1 > M3 — US oil storage economics favour prompt clearing except in acute contango windows.
  2. The 2026 regime is tempered on WTI. Crisis regime ≈ 13% of OOS days, vs 17% on Brent and 31% on LSGO. US-crude insulation from Hormuz is the mechanism — disrupted AG barrels are Brent-priced first and WTI-priced only if/when arb economics force US exports West.
  3. The 2020-04-20 negative print is a known artefact of May-2020 expiring contract intraday; ICE's WBS 1!-ICE rolls before expiry so our series doesn't capture it (M1 minimum in our data is $41.21). Event markers on the chart flag the date; readers aware of the expiring-contract context shouldn't expect to see −$37 on the rolling series.
  4. Tails are present but smaller than LSGO. Excess kurtosis 130 sounds enormous but is driven by ~20 extreme-return days (1%); removing those leaves a near-Gaussian distribution. Compare to LSGO's 62 ex-kurtosis with 24 extreme days — WTI's tails are individually larger but less frequent.

Structured identically to the LSGO notebook: term structure → anomaly detection → event timeline/dashboard → HMM regime + forecast.

1. Term structure — price, vol, the curve¶

Flat price + 20-day realised vol over the full sample, then forward curves (regime-coloured), then short-dated spreads. The 2020 Q2 contango is the defining WTI event of the sample — storage-to-capacity at Cushing triggered the negative-settlement dynamic; rolling data captures the aftermath but not the single intraday print. The 2022 and 2025-2026 backwardation windows match Brent directionally but at ~50% of the magnitude — the US-inland insulation in action.

In [6]:
fca.chart_price_vol();
In [7]:
fca.chart_forward_curves();
In [8]:
fca.chart_short_structure();
In [9]:
fca.chart_long_structure();
In [10]:
fca.chart_full_strip();

2. Anomaly detection — Z-scores and the distribution¶

IS-anchored Z (pre-2025 mean / std as ruler). Axis capped at ±5 for readability; annotated peak values surface the off-chart magnitudes. The distribution panel separates IS (Gaussian-tailed) from OOS (fat-tailed) — WTI's excess kurtosis 130 is driven by ~20 extreme-return days; remove those and the distribution is near-Gaussian.

In [11]:
fca.chart_zscore(SPREAD='M1_M3');
In [12]:
fca.chart_zscore(SPREAD='M1_M6');
In [13]:
fca.chart_distribution();

3. Events, dashboard, CDF regime label¶

Portfolio-wide event overlay on the spread series, then a 4-panel dashboard, then CDF regime classification using the pre-2025 80/95/99 percentiles on M1-M6 (Tight/Stress/Crisis thresholds 1.15 / 1.44 / 1.61 USD/bbl).

Note on 2020-04-20: the event line marks the date of the WTI negative settlement (expiring May-2020 contract, intraday −$37). ICE's rolling front-month `WBS 1!-ICE` rolls before expiry, so the rolling series does *not* show the negative print — our M1 minimum is $41.21. The event marker is a date, not a price point; readers aware of the expiring-contract context shouldn't expect to see −$37 on the rolling series.

In [14]:
fca.chart_event_timeline();
In [15]:
fca.chart_dashboard();
In [16]:
fca.chart_regime();

4. HMM probabilistic regime + next-day forecast¶

Same methodology as LSGO/Brent. HMM probabilities complement the CDF hard labels; the transition matrix gives next-day regime probabilities. In the current OOS window the stay-in-state-1 probability is ≈1, so the forecast horizon trivially extrapolates — informative only because it reveals the regime is persistent, not transient.

In [17]:
fca.chart_hmm_regime(SPREAD='M1_M6', N_STATES=2);

HMM means (by state): [ 0.44562533 16.64442367]
HMM std   (by state): [0.6557333  7.84476475]
Transition matrix:
[[9.99476848e-01 5.23152140e-04]
 [1.58580231e-15 1.00000000e+00]]
IS  P(crisis)>0.5 days: 0/1613 (0.0%)
OOS P(crisis)>0.5 days: 34/333 (10.2%)

Forward-looking forecast¶

From today's HMM state probabilities, iterate the transition matrix 5 business days forward. Non-zero P(crisis) tomorrow means the regime is still active — the transition matrix's stay-crisis probability is close to 1 during the 2026-Q2 OOS window.

In [18]:
fca.forecast_next_day_regime(HORIZON=5);

As of 2026-04-17:
  today  P(state0)=0.000 · P(state1)=1.000
  +1d    P(state0)=0.000 · P(state1)=1.000
  +2d    P(state0)=0.000 · P(state1)=1.000
  +3d    P(state0)=0.000 · P(state1)=1.000
  +4d    P(state0)=0.000 · P(state1)=1.000
  +5d    P(state0)=0.000 · P(state1)=1.000

Trader takeaways¶

  • WTI is the insulated crude. Same directional behaviour as Brent but ~50% of the magnitude during geopolitical events. When Hormuz is the shock (2026), trade Brent; when US-inland is the shock (Cushing congestion, PADD3 refinery outages), trade WTI.
  • Brent-WTI basin spread is the cleanest US-vs-world read. See ../Cross_Market_Analysis/ — at $7-8/bbl the arb opens for USGC crude to Europe, mechanically compressing the gap.
  • The 2020 negative print is a known artefact of the expiring May contract, not captured in our rolling M1 series. Cite the date, don't quote the −$37 on charts that only show the rolling series.
  • Regime thresholds (80/95/99 pct of pre-2025 M1-M6): Tight 1.15 · Stress 1.44 · Crisis 1.61 USD/bbl. OOS Crisis 13% — the mildest of the three commodities.
  • Tail frequency is low, magnitudes high. Use option structures for downside protection in crisis windows; linear hedges under-price the gap risk.
